context.pr_title: Fix: Prevent error in lldb `kernel vmmap`
context.pr_body:
The `kernel vmmap` command could raise an error in LLDB due to `kernel_vmmap()` not being fully compatible with the LLDB environment. Specifically, when `get_known_pages()` returned no pages, `LLDBProcess.vmmap()` would still attempt to call `kernel_vmmap()`, leading to an error when `KernelVmmap.adjust` tried to iterate over potentially invalid LLDBValue objects.

This commit modifies `LLDBProcess.vmmap()` to return an empty `LLDBMemoryMap` directly if `get_known_pages()` yields no results, thus avoiding the problematic call path in LLDB.

A new test has been added in `tests/lldb-tests/tests/test_command_vmmap.py` to verify that the `vmmap` command executes without error in LLDB and handles cases with no memory mappings gracefully.

Fixes #3102

<!--

    https://pwndbg.re/pwndbg/dev/contributing/ before creating a PR.
-->

[![Discord](https://img.shields.io/discord/843809097920413717?label=Discord&style=plastic)](https://discord.gg/x47DssnGwm)

`pwndbg` (/paʊnˈdiˌbʌɡ/) is a GDB and LLDB plug-in that makes debugging suck less,
with a focus on features needed by low-level software developers, hardware hackers,
reverse-engineers and exploit developers.

It has a boatload of features, see our [Features page](https://pwndbg.re/pwndbg/latest/features/)
and [CHEATSHEET][CHEATSHEET] (feel free to print it!). If you have any questions you may read the
[documentation](https://pwndbg.re/pwndbg/latest/) or asks us in our [Discord server](https://discord.gg/x47DssnGwm).

[CHEATSHEET]: https://pwndbg.re/pwndbg/dev/CHEATSHEET.pdf

## Why?

Vanilla GDB and LLDB are terrible to use for reverse engineering and exploit development.
Typing `x/30gx $rsp` or navigating cumbersome LLDB commands is not fun and often provides
minimal information. The year is 2025, and core debuggers still lack many user-friendly
features such as a robust hexdump command. WinDbg users are completely lost when they
occasionally need to bump into GDB or LLDB.

Pwndbg is a Python module which can be loaded into GDB or run as a REPL interface for LLDB.
It provides a suite of utilities and enhancements that fill the gaps left by these debuggers,
smoothing out rough edges and making them more user-friendly.

## Installation

See [installation instructions](https://pwndbg.re/pwndbg/latest/setup).

## What about ...?

Many past ([gdbinit][gdbinit], [PEDA][PEDA]) and present projects ([GEF][GEF],
[bata24/GEF][bata24/GEF]) offer great features, but are hard to extend and are packaged
as large single files ([103KB][gdbinit2], [195KB][peda.py], [423KB][gef.py],
[4.12MB][bata24/gef.py]). Pwndbg aims to replace them with a faster, cleaner, and
more robust implementation.

[gdbinit]: https://github.com/gdbinit/Gdbinit
[gdbinit2]: https://github.com/gdbinit/Gdbinit/blob/master/gdbinit
[PEDA]: https://github.com/longld/peda
[peda.py]: https://github.com/longld/peda/blob/master/peda.py
[GEF]: https://github.com/hugsy/gef
[gef.py]: https://github.com/hugsy/gef/blob/main/gef.py
[bata24/GEF]: https://github.com/bata24/gef
[bata24/gef.py]: https://github.com/bata24/gef/blob/dev/gef.py

Pwndbg supports both GDB and LLDB, and each debugger has its own strengths.

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file implements the LLDB backend for Pwndbg's debugger-agnostic API: wrappers around lldb.SB* objects (processes, threads, frames, values, types), execution control, memory access, symbol lookup, and command/event plumbing. It contains the LLDBProcess.vmmap implementation and related memory-map and memory-reading utilities the agent is looking for.

### Architecture
- Top-level helper functions: rename_register, map_type_code, _is_optimized_out.
- Lightweight wrappers for LLDB entities that implement the pwndbg.dbg_mod interfaces:
  - LLDBRegisters, LLDBFrame, LLDBThread, LLDBType, LLDBValue, LLDBMemoryMap, LLDBStopPoint, LLDBCommand.
- Execution controller types: OneShotAwaitable, YieldContinue, YieldSingleStep, LLDBExecutionController and a shared EXECUTION_CONTROLLER instance.
- Core process wrapper: LLDBProcess implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Process (memory ops, vmmap, symbol lookup, arch detection, remote IO).
- Debugger wrapper: LLDB implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Debugger (setup, commands, selection, event handling).
- The LLDBProcess.vmmap method tries to obtain the live memory map via get_known_pages (which queries lldb.SBProcess GetMemoryRegions). If that fails/returns empty, vmmap falls back to importing and using pwndbg.aglib.kernel.vmmap.kernel_vmmap and pwndbg.aglib.vmmap_custom.get_custom_pages to construct a MemoryMap.

Components interact as follows:
- LLDB (Debugger) creates LLDBProcess from lldb.SBDebugger state; LLDBProcess delegates many operations to the underlying lldb.SBProcess and lldb.SBTarget.
- LLDBValue/LLDBType wrap SBValue/SBType and provide dereference, casting, printing, and integer conversion used by higher-level Pwndbg features.
- vmmap-related operations live on LLDBProcess and return LLDBMemoryMap (a pwndbg.dbg_mod.MemoryMap subclass).

### Functions & Classes
- rename_register — translate certain register names (e.g., "eflags" -> "rflags") for LLDB; used by register access/write logic.
- map_type_code — map lldb.SBType type classes to pwndbg.dbg_mod.TypeCode values; used by LLDBType.code.
- _is_optimized_out — heuristically detect "optimized out" evaluations from lldb.SBValue.error.description; used by expression evaluation logic.

Classes and their methods (names preserved exactly):

- LLDBRegisters
  - __init__ — store groups and proc.
  - by_name — lookup a register by name across register groups, uses rename_register and returns LLDBValue or None. Important for implementing Frame.regs() lookups.

- LLDBFrame
  - __init__ — wrap lldb.SBFrame and proc.
  - lookup_symbol — try EvaluateExpression("&name") and fallback to proc.lookup_symbol; used when resolving symbols in the current frame.
  - evaluate_expression — call lldb.SBFrame.EvaluateExpression and wrap result as LLDBValue; checks _is_optimized_out and raises on error.
  - regs — return LLDBRegisters for the frame (calls self.inner.GetRegisters()).
  - reg_write — write register value; handles PC specially via SBFrame.SetPC, otherwise performs register write via LLDB command "register write ..."; uses selection context managers and triggers EventType.REGISTER_CHANGED. Important because LLDB register write requires special handling.
  - pc — return self.inner.GetPC()
  - sp — return self.inner.GetSP()
  - parent — return LLDBFrame wrapping parent frame if valid.
  - child — return child frame as LLDBFrame when present.
  - sal — return (file, line) from GetLineEntry if valid.
  - __eq__ — equality based on inner SBFrame.

- LLDBThread
  - __init__ — wrap lldb.SBThread and proc.
  - bottom_frame — contextmanager yielding the bottom frame (index 0) as LLDBFrame; raises if no frames.
  - ptid — return thread id via inner.id
  - index — return thread idx via inner.idx

- LLDBType
  - __init__ — wrap lldb.SBType.
  - __eq__ — compare inner types.
  - name_identifier (property) — return name unless anonymous.
  - name_to_human_readable (property) — human-readable name from inner.name.
  - sizeof (property) — inner.GetByteSize()
  - alignof (property) — use inner.GetByteAlign() on LLDB >= 19.1 otherwise default to sizeof.
  - code (property) — returns a pwndbg.dbg_mod.TypeCode via map_type_code (or INVALID on error).
  - func_arguments — return function argument types for function types.
  - fields — return type fields (handles ENUM and other member arrays).
  - array — GetArrayType(count) wrapper.
  - pointer — GetPointerType() wrapper.
  - strip_typedefs — follow typedef chain with GetTypedefedType().
  - target — return pointee or array element type; raises if neither.

- LLDBValue
  - __init__ — wrap lldb.SBValue and proc.
  - address (property) — AddressOf() wrapped as LLDBValue or None.
  - is_optimized_out (property) — use _is_optimized_out.
  - type (property) — return LLDBType for inner.type; asserts not optimized out.
  - dereference — Dereference handling with special cases for null pointers and creating SBData via SBTarget.CreateValueFromData if needed; reads memory using proc.read_memory for manual null-dereference behavior.
  - string — read C string from memory via ReadCStringFromMemory in growing buffers; returns last_str or raises Error.
  - value_to_human_readable — str(self.inner) (stringify SBValue).
  - fetch_lazy — no-op for LLDB.
  - __int__ — integer conversion; pointer uses GetValueAsUnsigned; numeric uses lldb.is_numeric_type.
  - cast — cast SBValue to LLDBType.inner via inner.Cast.
  - _self_add_sub_int — pointer arithmetic helper (used by __add__/__sub__); adjusts by element size.
  - __add__ — pointer addition wrapper.
  - __sub__ — pointer subtraction wrapper.
  - __getitem__ — child access by name or index; pointer index dereference implemented via offset arithmetic and Dereference().

- LLDBMemoryMap
  - __init__ — takes pages: List[pwndbg.lib.memory.Page] and delegates to parent.
  - is_qemu — returns False (placeholder for QEMU detection). This is the MemoryMap returned by LLDBProcess.vmmap.

- LLDBStopPoint
  - __init__ — stores inner breakpoint/watchpoint, proc, stop_handler_name.
  - remove — delete breakpoint/watchpoint and remove stop handler name symbol from module scope when set.
  - set_enabled — wrapper for inner.SetEnabled(enabled).

- OneShotAwaitable
  - __init__ — stores value to yield once.
  - __await__ — yields the stored value once. Used for execution controller awaits.

- YieldContinue — dataclass-like class storing target: LLDBStopPoint and selected_thread: bool; used as marker yielded to event loop.
- YieldSingleStep — marker for single-step continuation.

- LLDBExecutionController
  - single_step — returns OneShotAwaitable(YieldSingleStep()).
  - cont — assert target is LLDBStopPoint and return OneShotAwaitable(YieldContinue(target)).
  - cont_selected_thread — same but with selected_thread=True.

- EXECUTION_CONTROLLER — shared LLDBExecutionController instance.

- LLDBProcess
  - __init__ — store dbg, process, target, and _is_gdb_remote flag; initialize _created_value_serial.
  - threads — build list of LLDBThread for current process threads.
  - pid — return process id if alive() else None.
  - alive — check SBProcess state not Exited/Detached.
  - stopped_with_signal — check if any thread stop reason is signal.
  - evaluate_expression — target.EvaluateExpression wrapper returning LLDBValue; checks _is_optimized_out and errors.
  - get_known_pages — iterate regions = self.process.GetMemoryRegions(), for each region build pwndbg.lib.memory.Page with start, size, flags (read/write/exec mapped to os.R_OK/os.W_OK/os.X_OK), offset=0 (LLDB doesn't provide file offset), objfile resolution (resolve empty name via SBAddress.GetModule().GetFileSpec() or name like "[anon_...]" ), detect overlapping regions and skip duplicates using local 'ranges' and 'lens' sorted lists and bisect. Returns list of pwndbg.lib.memory.Page. This is the primary function that queries LLDB for mapped memory regions.
  - vmmap — call get_known_pages(); if non-empty return LLDBMemoryMap(pages); otherwise import pwndbg.aglib.kernel.vmmap.kernel_vmmap and pwndbg.aglib.vmmap_custom.get_custom_pages, extend pages with kernel_vmmap() + get_custom_pages(), sort and return LLDBMemoryMap(pages). This is the main method you want.
  - find_largest_range_len — binary-search helper to find readable memory range maximum length given a test callable; used by read_memory partial reads fallback.
  - read_memory — ReadMemory(address, size, e) wrapper. If exact read fails and partial=True, fallback strategies:
      - try to use cached aglib.vmmap.get() to figure contiguous vmmap_size and retry
      - otherwise binary-search using find_largest_range_len to find largest readable prefix and return it (or empty bytearray)
    Important for robust memory reads when LLDB can't read the full requested size.
  - write_memory — WriteMemory wrapper; triggers EventType.MEMORY_CHANGED.
  - find_in_memory — manual find implementation using lldb.SBProcess.ReadMemory in alignment-sized chunks; yields matching addresses. Used when LLDB lacks a higher-level FindRangesInMemory.
  - is_remote — return self._is_gdb_remote.
  - send_remote — send gdb-remote packet via LLDB command "process plugin packet send {packet}", parse output and return bytes. Requires _is_gdb_remote True.
  - send_monitor — use "process plugin packet monitor {cmd}" and trim response to match GDB semantics.
  - download_remote_file — wraps target.GetPlatform().Get(remote, local) via lldb.SBFileSpec; for remote file retrieval via LLDB platform.
  - create_value — create an SBData containing little-endian uint64 of `value` and use target.CreateValueFromData to make a temporary LLDBValue; increments _created_value_serial. Accepts optional type to cast to. Used widely for creating values representing addresses/integers to hand to other APIs.
  - symbol_name_at_address — ResolveSymbolContextForAddress with eSymbolContextEverything and return ctx.symbol.name if valid.
  - _resolve_tls_symbol — heuristics to resolve TLS symbol addresses on Linux/Glibc by locating TCB via pwndbg.aglib.tls finders and scanning dtv entries; returns candidate address or None. Used when LLDB reports symbols with no type (potential TLS).
  - lookup_symbol — high-level symbol lookup using _iter_symbols and preference logic (static vs external); returns self.create_value(resolved_addr, cast_type) or None.
  - _iter_symbols — core symbol iteration implementation: collects variable types (FindGlobalVariables), maps domains based on requested type (ANY/VARIABLE/FUNCTION), calls FindSymbols for each domain, yields tuples (sbsymbol, pwndbg type to cast to, resolved_addr). Handles TLS symbol sections (".tbss", ".tdata") via _resolve_tls_symbol. Important for LLDB-based symbol resolution.
  - types_with_name — target.FindTypes wrapper returning list of LLDBType.
  - arch — detect endianness and pointer size using process/target byte order and address byte size; map target triple CPU names to Pwndbg conventions (x86-64, armcm, aarch64->aarch64 etc) and return ArchDefinition(name, ptrsize, endian, Platform.LINUX). Important for architecture-specific behavior.
  - break_at — create breakpoints/watchpoints via target.BreakpointCreateByAddress or target.WatchAddress; handle registering stop handler by creating a Python function in the imported module namespace and adding it to LLDB breakpoint/watchpoint commands; returns LLDBStopPoint. Important for breakpoint automation and stop handlers.
  - disasm — ReadInstructions at address and return a pwndbg.dbg_mod.DisassembledInstruction dict with addr, asm, length.
  - is_linux — heuristics check for SysV ABI via target.GetABIName().
  - _resolve_fullpath — resolve symlinks for module file paths via pwndbg.aglib.file.readlink (llDB doesn't resolve symlinks automatically).
  - module_section_locations — iterate target modules and their sections to return list of (load, size, section_name, fullpath) for loaded sections.
  - main_module_name — return resolved path for module 0 file spec or None.
  - main_module_entry — return entry point load address for first module or None.
  - is_dynamically_linked — return target.GetNumModules() > 1 (heuristic).
  - dispatch_execution_controller — append coroutine to self.dbg.controllers to be executed by the Pwndbg CLI.
  - (Note: there are many methods implementing the pwndbg.dbg_mod.Process interface; above are the ones most relevant for vmmap and memory/symbol operations.)

- LLDBCommand
  - __init__ — store handler_name and command_name (used as handle for added commands).

- LLDB
  - setup — initialize state, disable pwnlib.update, load aglib and pwndbg commands, import pwndbg.dbg.lldb.hooks. Called by lldbinit.py to initialize the Pwndbg LLDB adapter.
  - _execute_lldb_command — run LLDB command via GetCommandInterpreter().HandleCommand and return output; raise pwndbg.dbg_mod.Error on failure.
  - add_command — wrap a Python handler in an LLDB command class, register it in module scope under name "__LLDB_COMMAND_{command_name}" and add via "command script add"; returns LLDBCommand handle. Important for integrating Pwndbg commands with LLDB.
  - history — placeholder returning [].
  - commands — placeholder returning [].
  - lex_args — uses shlex.split to parse command-line.
  - _any_inferior — select first target/process and return LLDBProcess or None; used by selected_inferior fallback logic.
  - selected_inferior — use exec_states stack or fall back to _any_inferior; return LLDBProcess or None.
  - _any_thread — pick a thread for selected_inferior preferring selected thread; return LLDBThread or None.
  - selected_thread — use exec_states or fall back to _any_thread.
  - _any_bottommost_frame — pick bottommost (index 0) frame or selected frame.
  - selected_frame — use exec_states stack or fallback.
  - has_event_type — declare unsupported event types for MEMORY_CHANGED and REGISTER_CHANGED (these are not supported as events in LLDB here).
  - event_handler — decorator to register event handlers in self.event_handlers for pwndbg.dbg_mod.EventType.
  - suspend_events — suspend an event.
  - resume_events — resume an event.
  - _fire_prompt_hook — call prompt_hook when REPL signals prompt hook.
  - _trigger_event — call registered handlers unless suspended; catches exceptions and uses pwndbg.exception.handler.
  - set_sysroot — set SDK root on target platform.
  - supports_breakpoint_creation_during_stop_handler — returns True.
  - breakpoint_locations — return list of enabled breakpoint location addresses for selected inferior.
  - name — returns pwndbg.dbg_mod.DebuggerType.LLDB.
  - x86_disassembly_flavor — query LLDB setting target.x86-disassembly-flavor and normalize to "att" or "intel".
  - string_limit — return 200.
  - get_cmd_window_size — return (None, None).
  - pre_ctx_lines (property) — return 0.
  - is_gdblib_available — return False.
  - addrsz — format address string: "%#16x".
  - set_python_diagnostics — no-op.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- LLDBProcess.vmmap — present in this file and is the principal method you were searching for. It first calls get_known_pages() and returns LLDBMemoryMap(pages) if pages are found. If get_known_pages() returns empty, vmmap falls back to importing and calling kernel_vmmap from pwndbg.aglib.kernel.vmmap and get_custom_pages from pwndbg.aglib.vmmap_custom, then sorts pages and returns LLDBMemoryMap.
- LLDBProcess.get_known_pages — implemented here; it uses lldb.SBProcess.GetMemoryRegions() and constructs pwndbg.lib.memory.Page entries, handling anonymous regions, overlapping regions, permissions, and inserting into a sorted ranges list. This is the live-LLDB path for vmmap.
- LLDBMemoryMap — this class (subclass of pwndbg.dbg_mod.MemoryMap) is constructed by LLDBProcess.vmmap and is the representation returned to the rest of Pwndbg — its is_qemu method returns False here.
- Kernel vmmap / KernelVmmap.adjust — There is no KernelVmmap class or KernelVmmap.adjust defined in this file. vmmap imports kernel_vmmap from pwndbg.aglib.kernel.vmmap (see vmmap method). If you need KernelVmmap.adjust or the kernel_vmmap implementation, inspect pwndbg/aglib/kernel/vmmap.py (the kernel-side vmmap implementation lives in aglib, not in this file).
- get_known_pages and read_memory are closely related: vmmap uses get_known_pages to obtain the live mapping; read_memory contains fallbacks that consult pwndbg.aglib.vmmap.get() (cached aglib vmmap) when partial reads are needed — so changes to vmmap or caching in aglib will affect read_memory’s behavior.
- If your planned PR will modify vmmap behavior, examine:
  - LLDBProcess.get_known_pages for the LLDB memory-region extraction logic (bisect overlap logic, objfile resolution via SBAddress/GetModule/GetFileSpec).
  - LLDBProcess.vmmap for fallback to kernel_vmmap and get_custom_pages when LLDB did not return pages.
  - pwndbg.aglib.kernel.vmmap and pwndbg.aglib.vmmap_custom modules (imported in vmmap) for kernel_vmmap implementation and any KernelVmmap.adjust functionality the agent expects.
- Also note interaction points: pwndbg.aglib.vmmap.get() is used by read_memory — editing vmmap data sources may require ensuring aglib.vmmap cache behavior remains consistent.

If you want, I can open/summarize pwndbg/aglib/kernel/vmmap.py and pwndbg/aglib/vmmap_custom.py next to locate KernelVmmap.adjust and the kernel_vmmap implementation referenced here.

### File Overview
This file implements the LLDB backend for Pwndbg's debugger-agnostic API: wrappers around lldb.SB* objects (processes, threads, frames, values, types), execution control, memory access, symbol lookup, and command/event plumbing. It contains the LLDBProcess.vmmap implementation and related memory-map and memory-reading utilities the agent is looking for.

### Architecture
- Top-level helper functions: rename_register, map_type_code, _is_optimized_out.
- Lightweight wrappers for LLDB entities that implement the pwndbg.dbg_mod interfaces:
  - LLDBRegisters, LLDBFrame, LLDBThread, LLDBType, LLDBValue, LLDBMemoryMap, LLDBStopPoint, LLDBCommand.
- Execution controller types: OneShotAwaitable, YieldContinue, YieldSingleStep, LLDBExecutionController and a shared EXECUTION_CONTROLLER instance.
- Core process wrapper: LLDBProcess implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Process (memory ops, vmmap, symbol lookup, arch detection, remote IO).
- Debugger wrapper: LLDB implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Debugger (setup, commands, selection, event handling).

If that fails/returns empty, vmmap falls back to importing and using pwndbg.aglib.kernel.vmmap.kernel_vmmap and pwndbg.aglib.vmmap_custom.get_custom_pages to construct a MemoryMap.

Components interact as follows:
- LLDB (Debugger) creates LLDBProcess from lldb.SBDebugger state; LLDBProcess delegates many operations to the underlying lldb.SBProcess and lldb.SBTarget.
- LLDBValue/LLDBType wrap SBValue/SBType and provide dereference, casting, printing, and integer conversion used by higher-level Pwndbg features.

- map_type_code — map lldb.SBType type classes to pwndbg.dbg_mod.TypeCode values; used by LLDBType.code.

- LLDBRegisters

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file provides utilities to build a kernel virtual memory map (vmmap) when debugging kernels running under QEMU. It can obtain mappings either by parsing guest page tables (via gdb-pt-dump/PageTableDump) or by asking the QEMU monitor for "info mem", and it post-processes pages into higher-level regions via the KernelVmmap class.

### Architecture
- Kernel vmmap retrieval has two primary backends:
  - kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables: uses a QemuMachine (Machine subclass) + PageTableDump backends (PT_x86_64_Backend, PT_Aarch64_Backend, PT_RiscV64_Backend) to parse guest page tables and produce pwndbg.lib.memory.Page objects.
  - kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem: parses `monitor info mem` output from QEMU.
- The selected source is controlled by the configuration parameter kernel_vmmap_mode.
- The top-level kernel_vmmap function picks a backend, optionally post-processes the returned pages by instantiating KernelVmmap and calling KernelVmmap.adjust, and returns the final tuple of pages.
- QemuMachine implements the Machine interface required by the PageTableDump library and provides physical memory reads by translating GPA to HVA via QEMU monitor calls and reading /proc/{qemu-pid}/mem.
- KernelVmmap is responsible for taking raw page entries and annotating them with semantic names (userland, kernel [.text], kernel [.rodata], etc.) and offsets, using kernel symbols / architecture heuristics when available.

### Functions & Classes
- `KernelVmmap` — class that annotates a sequence of pwndbg.lib.memory.Pages into semantic sections (user vs kernel, rodata/bss/driver/etc.) and computes offsets.
  - Class attributes:
    - `USERLAND`, `KERNELLAND`, `KERNELRO`, `KERNELBSS`, `KERNELDRIVER`, `ESPSTACK` — string constants used as object-file / section labels.
  - `__init__(self, pages: Tuple[pwndbg.lib.memory.Page, ...])` — constructs a KernelVmmap for a given tuple of pages.
    - Looks up kernel-related symbols and computes self.sections based on architecture:
      - On x86-64 it uses pwndbg.aglib.kernel.paging.find_kbase(pages) for kbase, and looks up vmalloc_base, vmemmap_base and physmap_base and uses hard-coded markers (e.g., cpu entry, EFI, fixmap, legacy abi) to create `self.sections`.
      - On aarch64 it reads the kernel symbol "address_markers" and reads memory values and names via pwndbg.aglib.memory.u64 and memory.string to build `self.sections`.
    - If debug symbols are not available (pwndbg.aglib.kernel.has_debug_syms() False), exits early (self.sections remains None).
    - Sets self.pages = pages and self.sections (or None).
    - Importance: this sets up the mapping from addresses to region names used later by adjust.
  - `get_name(self, addr: int) -> str` — finds which section name (from self.sections) an address belongs to, returning the name or None.
    - Importance: used by adjust to initially label pages by region based on address ranges.
  - `adjust(self)` — main post-processing routine that updates page.objfile and page.offset for each page.
    - Calls get_name for each page.start and sets page.objfile when name is not None.
    - Finds the first userland (USERLAND) index and first kernel (.text KERNELLAND) index, then calls:
      - `handle_user_pages(user_idx)`
      - `handle_kernel_pages(kernel_idx)`
      - `handle_offsets()`
    - Importance: this is the method referenced in the PR description; it is the entry point to update pages with semantic labels and offsets.
  - `handle_user_pages(self, user_idx)` — heuristics to distinguish userland subregions (heap vs stack vs library) by inspecting address deltas relative to base_offset. Modifies page.objfile accordingly.
    - Importance: refines USERLAND-labeled pages into more useful categories for display.
  - `handle_kernel_pages(self, kernel_idx)` — iterates kernel pages and:
    - Converts non-executable writable pages to KERNELBSS, non-executable non-writable to KERNELRO.
    - Uses self.kbase and page.execute to detect loadable drivers (KERNELDRIVER).
    - If the current stack register value is contained in a page, marks it "kernel [stack]" using the check `if pwndbg.aglib.regs[pwndbg.aglib.regs.stack] in page:`.
    - Importance: splits kernel region into .text, .rodata, .bss, driver, kernel stack, etc.
  - `handle_offsets(self)` — computes page.offset for each page by computing difference from the last base for each objfile; if the offset is too large (exceeding 8 hex digits) set offset to 0.
    - The check `if page.objfile != self.KERNELRO and prev_objfile != page.objfile` is applied so KERNELRO uses a stable base in some cases.
    - Importance: sets offsets used for display and symbol lookup convenience (e.g., for symbols such as init_creds).

- `QemuMachine` — class inheriting `Machine` (from pt.machine) that implements platform-specific methods required by PageTableDump to read physical memory and registers for a QEMU guest.
  - `__init__(self)` — opens /proc/{qemu-pid}/mem for reading. Uses `QemuMachine.get_qemu_pid()` to determine appropriate qemu-system process PID; stores self.pid and a file descriptor self.file.
    - May raise PermissionError if /proc/.../mem cannot be opened.
    - Importance: establishes the means to read guest physical memory from the host.
  - `__del__(self)` — closes self.file if opened.
  - `search_pids_for_file(pids: List[str], filename: str) -> str | None` — static method that scans /proc/{pid}/fd for a given filename to find which PID has that file open. Returns matching pid string or None.
    - Importance: helper used by get_qemu_pid to locate qemu process that opened a temporary chardev file.
  - `get_qemu_pid()` — static method to determine qemu-system process PID:
    - First tries `pgrep qemu-system` and if exactly one result, returns it.
    - If not found, uses a chardev trick: creates a NamedTemporaryFile, asks the selected inferior to `monitor chardev-add file,id=...,path=tmpname`, then uses search_pids_for_file on candidate pids; then removes chardev.
    - Raises ProcessLookupError if cannot find a pid.
    - Importance: robustly finds the qemu process that hosts the guest (used to open /proc/{pid}/mem).
  - `read_physical_memory(self, physical_address: int, length: int) -> bytes` — translates guest physical address to host virtual address by calling `pwndbg.dbg.selected_inferior().send_monitor("gpa2hva {hex(physical_address)}")`, parses the HVA from output, and uses os.pread on the opened /proc/{pid}/mem to read the memory. Breaks large reads into max_block_size chunks. Raises OSError with a helpful message on failure.
    - Importance: provides the raw physical memory reads needed by the PageTableDump parser.
  - `read_register(self, register_name: str) -> int` — returns the register value using pwndbg.aglib.regs; strips leading "$" if present: `return int(getattr(pwndbg.aglib.regs, register_name))`.
    - Importance: supplies PF/EH/CPU register values to the page-table parsing backend.

- `kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables()` — function (decorated with `@pwndbg.lib.cache.cache_until("stop")`) that:
  - Verifies pwndbg.aglib.qemu.is_qemu_kernel() and that platform is "linux".
  - Instantiates QemuMachine (may print errors and return empty tuple on PermissionError or ProcessLookupError).
  - Selects an arch backend (PT_Aarch64_Backend, PT_x86_64_Backend, PT_RiscV64_Backend) based on pwndbg.aglib.arch.name; prints an error and returns empty tuple if arch unsupported.
  - Checks pwndbg.aglib.kernel.paging_enabled() and returns empty if disabled.
  - Constructs a PageTableDump with the machine backend and arch_backend; calls arch_backend.parse_tables to obtain page entries.
  - Converts parsed page entries into pwndbg.lib.memory.Page objects with flags: read implied (4), write (2) if page.pwndbg_is_writeable(), execute (1) if page.pwndbg_is_executable(). Uses objfile names like f"[pt_{hex(start)[2:-3]}]".
  - Returns tuple(retpages).
  - Importance: primary code path for accurate vmmap reconstruction by parsing guest page tables.

- `monitor_info_mem_not_warned` — module-global boolean used to suppress repetitive warnings when the `monitor info mem` output has inconsistent size fields.

- `kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem()` — function (decorated with `@pwndbg.lib.cache.cache_until("stop")`) that:
  - Verifies pwndbg.aglib.qemu.is_qemu_kernel().
  - Calls `pwndbg.dbg.selected_inferior().send_monitor("info mem")` and handles exceptions and older QEMU versions returning "unknown command".
  - If the monitor output is "PG disabled", returns empty.
  - Parses each line of the monitor output of the form "start-end size perms" into start, end, size, permissions; warns once if end-start != size.
  - Translates permissions into flags (read=4, write=2, execute=1 if qemu prints an x field).
  - Appends pwndbg.lib.memory.Page(start, size, flags, 0, "<qemu>") entries and returns tuple(pages).
  - Importance: fallback or alternate vmmap source that relies on QEMU monitor rather than page-table parsing.

- `kernel_vmmap_mode` — config parameter added via `pwndbg.config.add_param` named "kernel-vmmap" with choices ["page-tables", "monitor", "none"], default "page-tables"; determines which retrieval method kernel_vmmap uses.

- `kernel_vmmap(process_pages=True) -> Tuple[pwndbg.lib.memory.Page, ...]` — high-level function that the rest of pwndbg likely calls for a kernel vmmap:
  - Returns empty tuple if not a QEMU kernel or arch unsupported.
  - Depending on kernel_vmmap_mode selects `kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables` or `kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem`.
  - For monitor mode on x86-64: if process_pages True, runs a pagewalk for each page via `pwndbg.aglib.kernel.paging.pagewalk(page.start)` and checks L4 entry sign bit to infer executable permissions and set page.flags |= 1 when appropriate.
  - If process_pages True and pages are present, constructs `kv = KernelVmmap(pages)` and calls `kv.adjust()` to annotate pages.
  - Returns tuple(pages).
  - Importance: this is the function the agent likely wants to find and is the one referenced by higher-level code to fetch kernel vmmap.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- The file directly contains the `kernel_vmmap` function the agent expects to find. The function is defined near the bottom and orchestrates selection between `kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables` and `kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem`, and—when process_pages is True—instantiates `KernelVmmap` and calls `KernelVmmap.adjust()` to post-process pages.
- The `KernelVmmap` class is defined at the top of the file (after imports) and contains the exact `adjust` method the agent is looking for. `adjust` is the entry point for labeling pages and computing offsets; it calls `get_name`, `handle_user_pages`, `handle_kernel_pages`, and `handle_offsets`.
- Interaction with LLDB / QEMU monitor and pwndbg internals:
  - QEMU monitor is used via `pwndbg.dbg.selected_inferior().send_monitor(...)` in `QemuMachine.read_physical_memory` (calls "gpa2hva ...") and in `kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem` (calls "info mem"). These are the primary LLDB/GDB monitor interactions.
  - KernelVmmap uses pwndbg symbols / memory helpers when building `self.sections`:
    - `pwndbg.aglib.kernel.has_debug_syms()` gate,

### File Overview
This file provides utilities to build a kernel virtual memory map (vmmap) when debugging kernels running under QEMU. It can obtain mappings either by parsing guest page tables (via gdb-pt-dump/PageTableDump) or by asking the QEMU monitor for "info mem", and it post-processes pages into higher-level regions via the KernelVmmap class.

### Architecture
- Kernel vmmap retrieval has two primary backends:
  - kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables: uses a QemuMachine (Machine subclass) + PageTableDump backends (PT_x86_64_Backend, PT_Aarch64_Backend, PT_RiscV64_Backend) to parse guest page tables and produce pwndbg.lib.memory.Page objects.
  - kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem: parses `monitor info mem` output from QEMU.
- The selected source is controlled by the configuration parameter kernel_vmmap_mode.
- The top-level kernel_vmmap function picks a backend, optionally post-processes the returned pages by instantiating KernelVmmap and calling KernelVmmap.adjust, and returns the final tuple of pages.
- QemuMachine implements the Machine interface required by the PageTableDump library and provides physical memory reads by translating GPA to HVA via QEMU monitor calls and reading /proc/{qemu-pid}/mem.
- KernelVmmap is responsible for taking raw page entries and annotating them with semantic names (userland, kernel [.text], kernel [.rodata], etc.) and offsets, using kernel symbols / architecture heuristics when available.

### Functions & Classes
- `KernelVmmap` — class that annotates a sequence of pwndbg.lib.memory.Pages into semantic sections (user vs kernel, rodata/bss/driver/etc.) and computes offsets.
  - Class attributes:
    - `USERLAND`, `KERNELLAND`, `KERNELRO`, `KERNELBSS`, `KERNELDRIVER`, `ESPSTACK` — string constants used as object-file / section labels.
  - `__init__(self, pages: Tuple[pwndbg.lib.memory.Page, ...])` — constructs a KernelVmmap for a given tuple of pages.
    - Looks up kernel-related symbols and computes self.sections based on architecture:
      - On x86-64 it uses pwndbg.aglib.kernel.paging.find_kbase(pages) for kbase, and looks up vmalloc_base, vmemmap_base and physmap_base and uses hard-coded markers (e.g., cpu entry, EFI, fixmap, legacy abi) to create `self.sections`.
      - On aarch64 it reads the kernel symbol "address_markers" and reads memory values and names via pwndbg.aglib.memory.u64 and memory.string to build `self.sections`.
    - If debug symbols are not available (pwndbg.aglib.kernel.has_debug_syms() False), exits early (self.sections remains None).
    - Sets self.pages = pages and self.sections (or None).
    - Importance: this sets up the mapping from addresses to region names used later by adjust.
  - `get_name(self, addr: int) -> str` — finds which section name (from self.sections) an address belongs to, returning the name or None.
    - Importance: used by adjust to initially label pages by region based on address ranges.
  - `adjust(self)` — main post-processing routine that updates page.objfile and page.offset for each page.
    - Calls get_name for each page.start and sets page.objfile when name is not None.
    - Finds the first userland (USERLAND) index and first kernel (.text KERNELLAND) index, then calls:
      - `handle_user_pages(user_idx)`
      - `handle_kernel_pages(kernel_idx)`
      - `handle_offsets()`
    - Importance: this is the method referenced in the PR description; it is the entry point to update pages with semantic labels and offsets.
  - `handle_user_pages(self, user_idx)` — heuristics to distinguish userland subregions (heap vs stack vs library) by inspecting address deltas relative to base_offset. Modifies page.objfile accordingly.
    - Importance: refines USERLAND-labeled pages into more useful categories for display.
  - `handle_kernel_pages(self, kernel_idx)` — iterates kernel pages and:
    - Converts non-executable writable pages to KERNELBSS, non-executable non-writable to KERNELRO.
    - Uses self.kbase and page.execute to detect loadable drivers (KERNELDRIVER).
    - If the current stack register value is contained in a page, marks it "kernel [stack]" using the check `if pwndbg.aglib.regs[pwndbg.aglib.regs.stack] in page:`.
    - Importance: splits kernel region into .text, .rodata, .bss, driver, kernel stack, etc.
  - `handle_offsets(self)` — computes page.offset for each page by computing difference from the last base for each objfile; if the offset is too large (exceeding 8 hex digits) set offset to 0.
    - The check `if page.objfile != self.KERNELRO and prev_objfile != page.objfile` is applied so KERNELRO uses a stable base in some cases.
    - Importance: sets offsets used for display and symbol lookup convenience (e.g., for symbols such as init_creds).

- `QemuMachine` — class inheriting `Machine` (from pt.machine) that implements platform-specific methods required by PageTableDump to read physical memory and registers for a QEMU guest.
  - `__init__(self)` — opens /proc/{qemu-pid}/mem for reading. Uses `QemuMachine.get_qemu_pid()` to determine appropriate qemu-system process PID; stores self.pid and a file descriptor self.file.
    - May raise PermissionError if /proc/.../mem cannot be opened.
    - Importance: establishes the means to read guest physical memory from the host.
  - `__del__(self)` — closes self.file if opened.
  - `search_pids_for_file(pids: List[str], filename: str) -> str | None` — static method that scans /proc/{pid}/fd for a given filename to find which PID has that file open. Returns matching pid string or None.
    - Importance: helper used by get_qemu_pid to locate qemu process that opened a temporary chardev file.
  - `get_qemu_pid()` — static method to determine qemu-system process PID:
    - First tries `pgrep qemu-system` and if exactly one result, returns it.
    - If not found, uses a chardev trick: creates a NamedTemporaryFile, asks the selected inferior to `monitor chardev-add file,id=...,path=tmpname`, then uses search_pids_for_file on candidate pids; then removes chardev.
    - Raises ProcessLookupError if cannot find a pid.
    - Importance: robustly finds the qemu process that hosts the guest (used to open /proc/{pid}/mem).
  - `read_physical_memory(self, physical_address: int, length: int) -> bytes` — translates guest physical address to host virtual address by calling `pwndbg.dbg.selected_inferior().send_monitor("gpa2hva {hex(physical_address)}")`, parses the HVA from output, and uses os.pread on the opened /proc/{pid}/mem to read the memory. Breaks large reads into max_block_size chunks. Raises OSError with a helpful message on failure.
    - Importance: provides the raw physical memory reads needed by the PageTableDump parser.
  - `read_register(self, register_name: str) -> int` — returns the register value using pwndbg.aglib.regs; strips leading "$" if present: `return int(getattr(pwndbg.aglib.regs, register_name))`.
    - Importance: supplies PF/EH/CPU register values to the page-table parsing backend.

- `kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables()` — function (decorated with `@pwndbg.lib.cache.cache_until("stop")`) that:
  - Verifies pwndbg.aglib.qemu.is_qemu_kernel() and that platform is "linux".
  - Instantiates QemuMachine (may print errors and return empty tuple on PermissionError or ProcessLookupError).
  - Selects an arch backend (PT_Aarch64_Backend, PT_x86_64_Backend, PT_RiscV64_Backend) based on pwndbg.aglib.arch.name; prints an error and returns empty tuple if arch unsupported.
  - Checks pwndbg.aglib.kernel.paging_enabled() and returns empty if disabled.
  - Constructs a PageTableDump with the machine backend and arch_backend; calls arch_backend.parse_tables to obtain page entries.
  - Converts parsed page entries into pwndbg.lib.memory.Page objects with flags: read implied (4), write (2) if page.pwndbg_is_writeable(), execute (1) if page.pwndbg_is_executable(). Uses objfile names like f"[pt_{hex(start)[2:-3]}]".
  - Returns tuple(retpages).
  - Importance: primary code path for accurate vmmap reconstruction by parsing guest page tables.

- `monitor_info_mem_not_warned` — module-global boolean used to suppress repetitive warnings when the `monitor info mem` output has inconsistent size fields.

- `kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem()` — function (decorated with `@pwndbg.lib.cache.cache_until("stop")`) that:
  - Verifies pwndbg.aglib.qemu.is_qemu_kernel().
  - Calls `pwndbg.dbg.selected_inferior().send_monitor("info mem")` and handles exceptions and older QEMU versions returning "unknown command".
  - If the monitor output is "PG disabled", returns empty.
  - Parses each line of the monitor output of the form "start-end size perms" into start, end, size, permissions; warns once if end-start != size.
  - Translates permissions into flags (read=4, write=2, execute=1 if qemu prints an x field).
  - Appends pwndbg.lib.memory.Page(start, size, flags, 0, "<qemu>") entries and returns tuple(pages).
  - Importance: fallback or alternate vmmap source that relies on QEMU monitor rather than page-table parsing.

- `kernel_vmmap_mode` — config parameter added via `pwndbg.config.add_param` named "kernel-vmmap" with choices ["page-tables", "monitor", "none"], default "page-tables"; determines which retrieval method kernel_vmmap uses.

- `kernel_vmmap(process_pages=True) -> Tuple[pwndbg.lib.memory.Page, ...]` — high-level function that the rest of pwndbg likely calls for a kernel vmmap:
  - Returns empty tuple if not a QEMU kernel or arch unsupported.
  - Depending on kernel_vmmap_mode selects `kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables` or `kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem`.
  - For monitor mode on x86-64: if process_pages True, runs a pagewalk for each page via `pwndbg.aglib.kernel.paging.pagewalk(page.start)` and checks L4 entry sign bit to infer executable permissions and set page.flags |= 1 when appropriate.
  - If process_pages True and pages are present, constructs `kv = KernelVmmap(pages)` and calls `kv.adjust()` to annotate pages.
  - Returns tuple(pages).
  - Importance: this is the function the agent likely wants to find and is the one referenced by higher-level code to fetch kernel vmmap.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal

The function is defined near the bottom and orchestrates selection between `kernel_vmmap_via_page_tables` and `kernel_vmmap_via_monitor_info_mem`, and—when process_pages is True—instantiates `KernelVmmap` and calls `KernelVmmap.adjust()` to post-process pages.

from __future__ import annotations

import tempfile

import gdb
import pytest

import pwndbg.aglib.proc
import tests

GAPS_MAP_BINARY = tests.binaries.get("mmap_gaps.out")
CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY = tests.binaries.get("crash_simple.out.hardcoded")
BINARY_ISSUE_1565 = tests.binaries.get("issue_1565.out")


def get_proc_maps():
    """
        Example info proc mappings:

    pwndbg> info proc mappings
    process 26781
    Mapped address spaces:

              Start Addr           End Addr       Size     Offset objfile
                0x400000           0x401000     0x1000        0x0 /opt/pwndbg/tests/gdb-tests/tests/binaries/crash_simple.out
          0x7ffff7ffa000     0x7ffff7ffd000     0x3000        0x0 [vvar]
          0x7ffff7ffd000     0x7ffff7fff000     0x2000        0x0 [vdso]
          0x7ffffffde000     0x7ffffffff000    0x21000        0x0 [stack]
      0xffffffffff600000 0xffffffffff601000     0x1000        0x0 [vsyscall]
    """
    maps = []

    # Note: info proc mappings may not have permissions information,
    # so we get it here and fill from `perms`
    with open("/proc/%d/maps" % pwndbg.aglib.proc.pid) as f:
        for line in f.read().splitlines():
            addrs, perms, offset, _inode, size, objfile = line.split(maxsplit=6)
            start, end = (int(v, 16) for v in addrs.split("-"))
            offset = offset.lstrip("0") or "0"
            size = end - start
            maps.append([hex(start), hex(end), perms, hex(size)[2:], offset, objfile])

    maps.sort()

    return maps


@pytest.mark.parametrize("unload_file", (False, True))
def test_command_vmmap_on_coredump_on_crash_simple_binary(start_binary, unload_file):
    """
    Example vmmap when debugging binary:
        LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA
                  0x400000           0x401000 r-xp     1000 0      /opt/pwndbg/tests/gdb-tests/tests/binaries/crash_simple.out
            0x7ffff7ffa000     0x7ffff7ffd000 r--p     3000 0      [vvar]
            0x7ffff7ffd000     0x7ffff7fff000 r-xp     2000 0      [vdso]
            0x7ffffffde000     0x7ffffffff000 rwxp    21000 0      [stack]
        0xffffffffff600000 0xffffffffff601000 r-xp     1000 0      [vsyscall]

    The same vmmap when debugging coredump:
        LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA
                  0x400000           0x401000 r-xp     1000 0      /opt/pwndbg/tests/gdb-tests/tests/binaries/crash_simple.out
            0x7ffff7ffd000     0x7ffff7fff000 r-xp     2000 1158   load2
            0x7ffffffde000     0x7ffffffff000 rwxp    21000 3158   [stack]
        0xffffffffff600000 0xffffffffff601000 r-xp     1000 24158  [vsyscall]

    Note that for a core-file, we display the [vdso] page as load2 and we are missing the [vvar] page.
    This is... how it is. It just seems that core files (at least those I met) have no info about
    the vvar page and also GDB can't access the [vvar] memory with its x/ command during core debugging.
    """
    start_binary(CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY)

    # Trigger binary crash
    gdb.execute("continue")

    expected_maps = get_proc_maps()

    gdb.execute("set vmmap-prefer-relpaths off")
    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap", to_string=True).splitlines()

    # Basic asserts
    assert len(vmmaps) == len(expected_maps) + 2  # +2 for header and legend
    assert vmmaps[0] == "LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA"

    # Split vmmaps
    vmmaps = [i.split() for i in vmmaps[2:]]

    # Assert that vmmap output matches expected one
    assert vmmaps == expected_maps

    # Now, generate core file, so we can then test coredump vmmap
    core = tempfile.mktemp()
    gdb.execute(f"generate-core-file {core}")

    # The test should work fine even if we unload the original binary
    if unload_file:
        gdb.execute("file")

    #### TEST COREDUMP VMMAP
    # Now, let's load the generated core file
    gdb.execute(f"core-file {core}")

    old_len_vmmaps = len(vmmaps)
    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap", to_string=True).splitlines()

    # Note: we will now see one less vmmap page as [vvar] will be missing
    assert vmmaps[0] == "LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA"
    vmmaps = [i.split() for i in vmmaps[2:]]

    has_proc_maps = "warning: unable to find mappings in core file" not in gdb.execute(
        "info proc mappings", to_string=True
    )

    if has_proc_maps:
        assert len(vmmaps) == old_len_vmmaps - 1
    else:
        # E.g. on Debian 10 with GDB 8.2.1 the core dump does not contain mappings info
        # (note: we don't support Debian 10 anymore, so this code may be removed in the future)
        assert len(vmmaps) == old_len_vmmaps - 2
        binary_map = next(i for i in expected_maps if CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY in i[-1])
        expected_maps.remove(binary_map)

    # Fix up expected maps
    next(i for i in expected_maps if i[-1] == "[vdso]")[-1] = "load2"

    vdso_map = next(i for i in expected_maps if i[-1] == "[vvar]")
    expected_maps.remove(vdso_map)

    def assert_maps():
        for vmmap, expected_map in zip(vmmaps, expected_maps):
            # On different Ubuntu versions, we end up with different results
            # Ubuntu 18.04*: vmmap.objfile for binary vmmap has binary file path
            # Ubuntu 22.04: the same vmmap is named as 'loadX'
            # The difference comes from the fact that the `info proc mappings`
            # command returns different results on the two.
            # It may be a result of different test binary compilation or
            # just a difference between GDB versions
            #
            # Another difference may occur for the vsyscall memory page:
            # on Ubuntu 22.04, while vsyscall is ---xp during debugging
            # it becomes r-xp and can be readable when we target the coredump
            # Likely, this is because on x86/x64 you can't set memory to be
            # eXecute only, and maybe generate-core-file was able to dump it?
            #
            # *NOTE: Ubuntu 18.04 is not supported anymore; leaving this code here
            # but feel free to remove it in the future if it is not needed anymore
            # for future versions
            if vmmap[-1] == expected_map[-1] == "[vsyscall]":
                assert vmmap[:2] == expected_map[:2]  # start, end
                assert vmmap[3] == expected_map[3] or vmmap[3] in ("r-xp", "--xp")
                assert vmmap[4:] == expected_map[4:]
                continue

            assert vmmap[:-1] == expected_map[:-1]
            if vmmap[-1].startswith("load"):
                continue
            assert vmmap[-1] == expected_map[-1]

    assert_maps()

    # Now also make sure that everything works fine if we remove
    # file symbols information from GDB; during writing this test
    # a bug with this popped out, so I am double checking it here
    gdb.execute("file")

    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap", to_string=True).splitlines()
    vmmaps = [i.split() for i in vmmaps[2:]]

    assert_maps()


def test_vmmap_issue_1565(start_binary):
    """
    https://github.com/pwndbg/pwndbg/issues/1565

    In tests this bug is reported as:
    >       gdb.execute("context")
    E       gdb.error: Error occurred in Python: maximum recursion depth exceeded in comparison

    In a normal GDB session this is reported as:
        Exception occurred: context: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object (<class 'RecursionError'>)
    """
    gdb.execute(f"file {BINARY_ISSUE_1565}")
    gdb.execute("break thread_function")
    gdb.execute("run")
    gdb.execute("next")
    gdb.execute("context")


def test_vmmap_gaps_option(start_binary):
    start_binary(GAPS_MAP_BINARY)

    gdb.execute("break break_here")
    gdb.execute("continue")

    # Test vmmap with gap option
    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap --gaps", to_string=True).splitlines()
    seen_gap = False
    seen_adjacent = False
    seen_guard = False
    # Skip the first line since the legend has gard and
    for line in vmmaps[1:]:
        if "GAP" in line:
            seen_gap = True
        if "ADJACENT" in line:
            seen_adjacent = True
        if "GUARD" in line:
            seen_guard = True
    assert seen_gap
    assert seen_adjacent
    assert seen_guard

### File Overview
This file contains pytest tests for the pwndbg "vmmap" command in GDB. It exercises regular debugging and core-file flows, validates vmmap output formats and contents (including special cases like missing pages in core files and gaps reporting), and reproduces a previously reported recursion bug (issue 1565).

### Architecture
- Top-level constants GAPS_MAP_BINARY, CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY, and BINARY_ISSUE_1565 point to test binaries supplied by the test harness.
- Utility function get_proc_maps() reads the current process /proc/<pid>/maps and converts it into the same tabular/list form that the tests expect from the vmmap output.
- The main test functions call into GDB via gdb.execute(...) to run binaries, trigger crashes, generate core files, switch to core files, and call the vmmap command. They then parse and compare vmmap output to the expected maps produced by get_proc_maps() with adjustments for known platform/GDB differences.
- An inner helper function assert_maps() (defined inside test_command_vmmap_on_coredump_on_crash_simple_binary) centralizes per-line comparison logic and special-case handling for vsyscall and "loadX" renaming of vdso entries.
- Tests rely on pytest fixtures (e.g., start_binary) and pwndbg.aglib.proc.pid for the pid used by get_proc_maps().

### Functions & Classes
get_proc_maps — Utility function that reads /proc/<pid>/maps for the process identified by pwndbg.aglib.proc.pid. It:
- Parses each line into start address, end address, permissions, size (computed), offset (normalized), and objfile fields.
- Returns a sorted list of entries with fields formatted similarly to the vmmap output (hex strings for start/end, perms, hex size string without "0x" prefix, offset, objfile).
Importance: Provides the "expected" mapping list against which vmmap output is validated; accounts for the fact that gdb's "info proc mappings" may not include permissions so this function reads the kernel maps directly.

test_command_vmmap_on_coredump_on_crash_simple_binary — Parametrized pytest test (unload_file: False/True) that:
- Uses the start_binary fixture to launch CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY, runs it to crash, then obtains expected_maps via get_proc_maps().
- Forces "set vmmap-prefer-relpaths off", calls "vmmap", splits and tokenizes output, and asserts structure: header equals "LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA", total lines equal len(expected_maps)+2, and that the tabular entries match expected_maps.
- Generates a core file via generate-core-file, optionally unlinks symbols via "file" (when unload_file=True), then opens the core with "core-file" and re-runs vmmap; accounts for missing [vvar] in core dumps and for [vdso] being renamed to "load2".
- Queries "info proc mappings" to see whether mappings exist in the core; uses that to choose between two different expected length adjustments.
- Mutates expected_maps to replace "[vdso]" entry name with "load2" and removes the "[vvar]" entry before comparing.
- Defines an inner helper assert_maps() to compare vmmap output lines to expected_maps with special-case logic:

summarization.user_prompt:
## File: tests/gdb-tests/tests/test_command_vmmap.py

## Agent's reason for reading this file:
Compare existing gdb vmmap test to proposed LLDB test to see what's needed

## What the agent expects to find:
Test cases and expectations for vmmap command, behaviors when no pages are returned, and how command's output is validated

## Source Code:
from __future__ import annotations

import tempfile

import gdb
import pytest

import pwndbg.aglib.proc
import tests

GAPS_MAP_BINARY = tests.binaries.get("mmap_gaps.out")
CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY = tests.binaries.get("crash_simple.out.hardcoded")
BINARY_ISSUE_1565 = tests.binaries.get("issue_1565.out")


def get_proc_maps():
    """
        Example info proc mappings:

    pwndbg> info proc mappings
    process 26781
    Mapped address spaces:

              Start Addr           End Addr       Size     Offset objfile
                0x400000           0x401000     0x1000        0x0 /opt/pwndbg/tests/gdb-tests/tests/binaries/crash_simple.out
          0x7ffff7ffa000     0x7ffff7ffd000     0x3000        0x0 [vvar]
          0x7ffff7ffd000     0x7ffff7fff000     0x2000        0x0 [vdso]
          0x7ffffffde000     0x7ffffffff000    0x21000        0x0 [stack]
      0xffffffffff600000 0xffffffffff601000     0x1000        0x0 [vsyscall]
    """
    maps = []

    # Note: info proc mappings may not have permissions information,
    # so we get it here and fill from `perms`
    with open("/proc/%d/maps" % pwndbg.aglib.proc.pid) as f:
        for line in f.read().splitlines():
            addrs, perms, offset, _inode, size, objfile = line.split(maxsplit=6)
            start, end = (int(v, 16) for v in addrs.split("-"))
            offset = offset.lstrip("0") or "0"
            size = end - start
            maps.append([hex(start), hex(end), perms, hex(size)[2:], offset, objfile])

    maps.sort()

    return maps


@pytest.mark.parametrize("unload_file", (False, True))
def test_command_vmmap_on_coredump_on_crash_simple_binary(start_binary, unload_file):
    """
    Example vmmap when debugging binary:
        LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA
                  0x400000           0x401000 r-xp     1000 0      /opt/pwndbg/tests/gdb-tests/tests/binaries/crash_simple.out
            0x7ffff7ffa000     0x7ffff7ffd000 r--p     3000 0      [vvar]
            0x7ffff7ffd000     0x7ffff7fff000 r-xp     2000 0      [vdso]
            0x7ffffffde000     0x7ffffffff000 rwxp    21000 0      [stack]
        0xffffffffff600000 0xffffffffff601000 r-xp     1000 0      [vsyscall]

    The same vmmap when debugging coredump:
        LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA
                  0x400000           0x401000 r-xp     1000 0      /opt/pwndbg/tests/gdb-tests/tests/binaries/crash_simple.out
            0x7ffff7ffd000     0x7ffff7fff000 r-xp     2000 1158   load2
            0x7ffffffde000     0x7ffffffff000 rwxp    21000 3158   [stack]
        0xffffffffff600000 0xffffffffff601000 r-xp     1000 24158  [vsyscall]

    Note that for a core-file, we display the [vdso] page as load2 and we are missing the [vvar] page.
    This is... how it is. It just seems that core files (at least those I met) have no info about
    the vvar page and also GDB can't access the [vvar] memory with its x/ command during core debugging.
    """
    start_binary(CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY)

    # Trigger binary crash
    gdb.execute("continue")

    expected_maps = get_proc_maps()

    gdb.execute("set vmmap-prefer-relpaths off")
    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap", to_string=True).splitlines()

    # Basic asserts
    assert len(vmmaps) == len(expected_maps) + 2  # +2 for header and legend
    assert vmmaps[0] == "LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA"

    # Split vmmaps
    vmmaps = [i.split() for i in vmmaps[2:]]

    # Assert that vmmap output matches expected one
    assert vmmaps == expected_maps

    # Now, generate core file, so we can then test coredump vmmap
    core = tempfile.mktemp()
    gdb.execute(f"generate-core-file {core}")

    # The test should work fine even if we unload the original binary
    if unload_file:
        gdb.execute("file")

    #### TEST COREDUMP VMMAP
    # Now, let's load the generated core file
    gdb.execute(f"core-file {core}")

    old_len_vmmaps = len(vmmaps)
    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap", to_string=True).splitlines()

    # Note: we will now see one less vmmap page as [vvar] will be missing
    assert vmmaps[0] == "LEGEND: STACK | HEAP | CODE | DATA | WX | RODATA"
    vmmaps = [i.split() for i in vmmaps[2:]]

    has_proc_maps = "warning: unable to find mappings in core file" not in gdb.execute(
        "info proc mappings", to_string=True
    )

    if has_proc_maps:
        assert len(vmmaps) == old_len_vmmaps - 1
    else:
        # E.g. on Debian 10 with GDB 8.2.1 the core dump does not contain mappings info
        # (note: we don't support Debian 10 anymore, so this code may be removed in the future)
        assert len(vmmaps) == old_len_vmmaps - 2
        binary_map = next(i for i in expected_maps if CRASH_SIMPLE_BINARY in i[-1])
        expected_maps.remove(binary_map)

    # Fix up expected maps
    next(i for i in expected_maps if i[-1] == "[vdso]")[-1] = "load2"

    vdso_map = next(i for i in expected_maps if i[-1] == "[vvar]")
    expected_maps.remove(vdso_map)

    def assert_maps():
        for vmmap, expected_map in zip(vmmaps, expected_maps):
            # On different Ubuntu versions, we end up with different results
            # Ubuntu 18.04*: vmmap.objfile for binary vmmap has binary file path
            # Ubuntu 22.04: the same vmmap is named as 'loadX'
            # The difference comes from the fact that the `info proc mappings`
            # command returns different results on the two.
            # It may be a result of different test binary compilation or
            # just a difference between GDB versions
            #
            # Another difference may occur for the vsyscall memory page:
            # on Ubuntu 22.04, while vsyscall is ---xp during debugging
            # it becomes r-xp and can be readable when we target the coredump
            # Likely, this is because on x86/x64 you can't set memory to be
            # eXecute only, and maybe generate-core-file was able to dump it?
            #
            # *NOTE: Ubuntu 18.04 is not supported anymore; leaving this code here
            # but feel free to remove it in the future if it is not needed anymore
            # for future versions
            if vmmap[-1] == expected_map[-1] == "[vsyscall]":
                assert vmmap[:2] == expected_map[:2]  # start, end
                assert vmmap[3] == expected_map[3] or vmmap[3] in ("r-xp", "--xp")
                assert vmmap[4:] == expected_map[4:]
                continue

            assert vmmap[:-1] == expected_map[:-1]
            if vmmap[-1].startswith("load"):
                continue
            assert vmmap[-1] == expected_map[-1]

    assert_maps()

    # Now also make sure that everything works fine if we remove
    # file symbols information from GDB; during writing this test
    # a bug with this popped out, so I am double checking it here
    gdb.execute("file")

    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap", to_string=True).splitlines()
    vmmaps = [i.split() for i in vmmaps[2:]]

    assert_maps()


def test_vmmap_issue_1565(start_binary):
    """
    https://github.com/pwndbg/pwndbg/issues/1565

    In tests this bug is reported as:
    >       gdb.execute("context")
    E       gdb.error: Error occurred in Python: maximum recursion depth exceeded in comparison

    In a normal GDB session this is reported as:
        Exception occurred: context: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object (<class 'RecursionError'>)
    """
    gdb.execute(f"file {BINARY_ISSUE_1565}")
    gdb.execute("break thread_function")
    gdb.execute("run")
    gdb.execute("next")
    gdb.execute("context")


def test_vmmap_gaps_option(start_binary):
    start_binary(GAPS_MAP_BINARY)

    gdb.execute("break break_here")
    gdb.execute("continue")

    # Test vmmap with gap option
    vmmaps = gdb.execute("vmmap --gaps", to_string=True).splitlines()
    seen_gap = False
    seen_adjacent = False
    seen_guard = False
    # Skip the first line since the legend has gard and
    for line in vmmaps[1:]:
        if "GAP" in line:
            seen_gap = True
        if "ADJACENT" in line:
            seen_adjacent = True
        if "GUARD" in line:
            seen_guard = True
    assert seen_gap
    assert seen_adjacent
    assert seen_guard

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file implements the LLDB backend for Pwndbg's debugger-agnostic API: it maps LLDB SB* objects (processes, threads, frames, values, types, etc.) to Pwndbg abstractions and provides process/memory/symbol/command facilities. It contains the full implementations of LLDB-specific classes and functions used across Pwndbg when running under LLDB.

### Architecture
- Top-level helper functions: rename_register, map_type_code, _is_optimized_out.
- Wrapper classes mapping LLDB SB* objects to pwndbg.dbg_mod interfaces:
  - LLDBRegisters, LLDBFrame, LLDBThread, LLDBType, LLDBValue, LLDBMemoryMap, LLDBStopPoint.
- Execution control classes: OneShotAwaitable, YieldContinue, YieldSingleStep, LLDBExecutionController, and a shared EXECUTION_CONTROLLER.
- Main process and debugger wrappers: LLDBProcess (implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Process) and LLDB (implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Debugger).
- Utilities for symbol/type lookup, memory reading/writing/searching, creating temporary LLDB values, and breakpoint/watchpoint handling.
- LLDBProcess.vmmap is implemented in terms of get_known_pages() with a fallback to aglib kernel_vmmap and get_custom_pages when no pages are returned by LLDB.

Objects interact as follows: LLDB provides debugger-level operations (commands, event management, selection). LLDBProcess wraps a specific lldb.SBProcess / lldb.SBTarget and implements memory/symbol/architecture operations. LLDBFrame/LLDBThread/LLDBValue/LLDBType are used by higher-level UI/commands to inspect and manipulate program state. ExecutionController classes are used for async execution control in the CLI.

### Functions & Classes
- T (TypeVar) — generic type variable used in type annotations.
- LLDB_VERSION: Tuple[int, int] — module-level variable to store LLDB version gating.

- rename_register — Maps register names from Pwndbg/GDB convention to LLDB names (special-cases "eflags" -> "rflags" for x86-64); used by register lookup/write helpers.

Class LLDBRegisters(pwndbg.dbg_mod.Registers)
- __init__ — Stores lldb.SBValueList groups and a LLDBProcess proc.
- by_name — Looks up a register by name across the groups; uses rename_register and returns LLDBValue or None. Important for generic register reads.

Class LLDBFrame(pwndbg.dbg_mod.Frame)
- __init__ — Wraps an lldb.SBFrame and associated LLDBProcess.
- lookup_symbol — Attempts to resolve a symbol by using evaluate_expression("&name") then falls back to proc.lookup_symbol; validates symbol characters. Important as frame-local symbol lookup may differ per arch (TLS fallback).
- evaluate_expression — Calls self.inner.EvaluateExpression(expression), checks for errors and optimized-out values, returns LLDBValue or raises pwndbg.dbg_mod.Error.
- regs — Returns LLDBRegisters from SBFrame.GetRegisters().
- reg_write — Writes a register name to value val. Handles PC specially via SBFrame.SetPC. For other registers, does a complex selection flow using selection context managers to ensure the right target/thread/frame are selected, runs "register write {name} {val}" command, triggers REGISTER_CHANGED event, verifies write by reading back. Important: uses command-based register writes due to LLDB private API limitations.
- pc — Returns self.inner.GetPC().
- sp — Returns self.inner.GetSP().
- parent — Returns parent frame as LLDBFrame or None.
- child — Returns child (callee) frame as LLDBFrame or None.
- sal — Returns (file, line) from SBFrame.GetLineEntry(), or None.
- __eq__ — Compares frames by inner SBFrame equality.

Class LLDBThread(pwndbg.dbg_mod.Thread)
- __init__ — Wraps lldb.SBThread and LLDBProcess.
- bottom_frame — Contextmanager yielding the bottom-most frame; raises if none.
- ptid — Returns thread id (inner.id).
- index — Returns thread index (inner.idx).

- map_type_code — Maps lldb.SBType TypeClass and flags to pwndbg.dbg_mod.TypeCode; used by LLDBType.code.

- _is_optimized_out — Heuristic to detect "optimized out" error messages from lldb.SBValue error descriptions. Used by expression evaluation to distinguish optimized-out vs invalid expressions.

Class LLDBType(pwndbg.dbg_mod.Type)
- __init__ — Stores lldb.SBType inner.
- __eq__ — Compares by inner.
- name_identifier (property) — Returns None for anonymous types else inner.name.
- name_to_human_readable (property) — Returns inner.name.
- sizeof (property) — Returns inner.GetByteSize().
- alignof (property) — Returns alignment using GetByteAlign if LLDB_VERSION supports it; otherwise defaults to sizeof.
- code (property) — Returns TypeCode via map_type_code, falls back to INVALID on exception.
- func_arguments — For function types, returns argument types as list of LLDBType.
- fields — Returns fields for struct/union/enum as list of pwndbg.dbg_mod.TypeField; handles enum members via get_enum_members_array() and fields via get_fields_array(), populating offsets and sizes.
- array — Returns LLDBType for array of count via GetArrayType.
- pointer — Returns LLDBType for pointer type via GetPointerType.
- strip_typedefs — Strips typedef chain returning underlying LLDBType.
- target — Returns pointee or array element type as LLDBType; raises on non-pointer/array.

Class LLDBValue(pwndbg.dbg_mod.Value)
- __init__ — Wraps lldb.SBValue and LLDBProcess.
- address (property) — Uses inner.AddressOf() returning LLDBValue or None.
- is_optimized_out (property) — Uses _is_optimized_out(self.inner).
- type (property) — Asserts not optimized out and returns LLDBType(self.inner.type).
- dereference — Implements dereference semantics: uses inner.Dereference(), checks errors, handles null pointer dereferences manually for LLDB versions refusing to dereference, constructs SBData and CreateValueFromData as workaround, returns LLDBValue. Important for safe pointer derefs even when LLDB refuses.
- string — Reads a C string by reading memory progressively (exponential buffer doubling) up to 4GB using SBProcess.ReadCStringFromMemory; returns last read string or raises on errors.
- value_to_human_readable — Returns str(self.inner).
- fetch_lazy — No-op under LLDB.
- __int__ — Converts SBValue to int: pointers use GetValueAsUnsigned, numeric types use LLDB helpers for signedness.

### File Overview
This file implements the LLDB backend for Pwndbg's debugger-agnostic API: it maps LLDB SB* objects (processes, threads, frames, values, types, etc.) to Pwndbg abstractions and provides process/memory/symbol/command facilities. It contains the full implementations of LLDB-specific classes and functions used across Pwndbg when running under LLDB.

### Architecture
- Top-level helper functions: rename_register, map_type_code, _is_optimized_out.
- Wrapper classes mapping LLDB SB* objects to pwndbg.dbg_mod interfaces:
  - LLDBRegisters, LLDBFrame, LLDBThread, LLDBType, LLDBValue, LLDBMemoryMap, LLDBStopPoint.
- Execution control classes: OneShotAwaitable, YieldContinue, YieldSingleStep, LLDBExecutionController, and a shared EXECUTION_CONTROLLER.
- Main process and debugger wrappers: LLDBProcess (implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Process) and LLDB (implements pwndbg.dbg_mod.Debugger).
- Utilities for symbol/type lookup, memory reading/writing/searching, creating temporary LLDB values, and breakpoint/watchpoint handling.
- LLDBProcess.vmmap is implemented in terms of get_known_pages() with a fallback to aglib kernel_vmmap and get_custom_pages when no pages are returned by LLDB.

Objects interact as follows: LLDB provides debugger-level operations (commands, event management, selection). LLDBProcess wraps a specific lldb.SBProcess / lldb.SBTarget and implements memory/symbol/architecture operations. LLDBFrame/LLDBThread/LLDBValue/LLDBType are used by higher-level UI/commands to inspect and manipulate program state. ExecutionController classes are used for async execution control in the CLI.

### Functions & Classes
- T (TypeVar) — generic type variable used in type annotations.
- LLDB_VERSION: Tuple[int, int] — module-level variable to store LLDB version gating.

- rename_register — Maps register names from Pwndbg/GDB convention to LLDB names (special-cases "eflags" -> "rflags" for x86-64); used by register lookup/write helpers.

Class LLDBRegisters(pwndbg.dbg_mod.Registers)
- __init__ — Stores lldb.SBValueList groups and a LLDBProcess proc.
- by_name — Looks up a register by name across the groups; uses rename_register and returns LLDBValue or None. Important for generic register reads.

Class LLDBFrame(pwndbg.dbg_mod.Frame)
- __init__ — Wraps an lldb.SBFrame and associated LLDBProcess.
- lookup_symbol — Attempts to resolve a symbol by using evaluate_expression("&name") then falls back to proc.lookup_symbol; validates symbol characters. Important as frame-local symbol lookup may differ per arch (TLS fallback).
- evaluate_expression — Calls self.inner.EvaluateExpression(expression), checks for errors and optimized-out values, returns LLDBValue or raises pwndbg.dbg_mod.Error.
- regs — Returns LLDBRegisters from SBFrame.GetRegisters().
- reg_write — Writes a register name to value val. Handles PC specially via SBFrame.SetPC. For other registers, does a complex selection flow using selection context managers to ensure the right target/thread/frame are selected, runs "register write {name} {val}" command, triggers REGISTER_CHANGED event, verifies write by reading back. Important: uses command-based register writes due to LLDB private API limitations.
- pc — Returns self.inner.GetPC().
- sp — Returns self.inner.GetSP().
- parent — Returns parent frame as LLDBFrame or None.
- child — Returns child (callee) frame as LLDBFrame or None.
- sal — Returns (file, line) from SBFrame.GetLineEntry(), or None.
- __eq__ — Compares frames by inner SBFrame equality.
